file_name: Jagtap et al. - 2026 - Skin cancer detection using deep learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a MobileNetV2 convolutional neural network model enhanced with transfer learning, to classify dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset into seven skin lesion categories for skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and evaluation of a MobileNetV2 deep learning model for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (71.38% accuracy, 85.77% precision, 60.42% recall) and describes a novel implementation pipeline including Flask-based web deployment, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning
journal: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based diagnostic system for skin cancer detection using the MobileNetV2 convolutional neural network enhanced with transfer learning. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven distinct skin lesion categories. The implementation pipeline included image preprocessing, model training with an Adam optimizer, and deployment via a Flask-based web application for real-time analysis. Evaluation on the test set yielded an accuracy of 71.38%, a mean precision of 85.77%, and a recall of 60.42%. The results indicate that while the system offers high precision for targeted lesion categories, challenges remain regarding class imbalance and recall rates. The authors conclude that the tool serves as an effective auxiliary aid for preliminary screening but requires further validation on heterogeneous datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven distinct skin lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Specifically mentioned in the context of a prediction result example with 99.73% confidence.

paper_terms: seven distinct categories of skin lesions
lesion_types_studied.details: The paper states the model classifies images into seven distinct categories from the HAM10000 dataset but does not explicitly list all seven specific labels (e.g., nevus, dermatofibroma, etc.) in the provided text, only mentioning melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma by name.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion categories (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, etc.) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories using MobileNetV2 with transfer learning. The model outputs class labels and associated confidence probabilities (e.g., 99.73% for Melanoma) via a softmax activation layer. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (71.38%), precision (85.77%), and recall (60.42%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image collection comprising 10,015 images across seven distinct skin lesion categories, sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive.
classes_used: seven distinct categories of skin lesions

use.training: Used for training the MobileNetV2 model with an 80:20 train-test split.
use.validation: Used for validation (20% split of the original dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the model's performance metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall) and a confusion matrix without statistical inference.

HAM10000.notes: The model outperformed traditional SVM and basic CNN approaches in classification accuracy. A specific prediction example showed a confidence level of 99.73% for Melanoma. The lower recall is attributed to class imbalance within the dataset and morphological resemblances among lesion variants.

custom_layers: Final Dense layer with Softmax activation
architecture_details.implementation_notes: Transfer learning applied; base layers frozen; custom classification layers added

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as a preprocessing step applied to the HAM10000 dataset to optimize image quality and bolster framework robustness. The paper does not report specific geometric transforms, color transforms, probabilities, ranges, or filtering thresholds used.

conclusions: The proposed MobileNetV2 model enhanced with transfer learning achieved a classification accuracy of 71.38%, a mean precision of 85.77%, and a recall of 60.42% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The system outperformed traditional Support Vector Machine (SVM) and basic Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) approaches in terms of classification accuracy.

The elevated precision indicates the model's capacity to deliver dependable identifications for targeted lesion categories, while the lower recall is attributed to class imbalance within the dataset and morphological resemblances among lesion variants.
Transfer learning reduced training time significantly while maintaining feature quality.
The framework was successfully deployed via a Flask-based web application, enabling seamless real-time deployment and rapid automated assessments for preliminary screening.
The system serves as an auxiliary aid for preliminary screening and must not supplant expert clinical evaluation.
The model's efficacy relies substantially on dataset quality and distributional equity; class imbalances in the HAM10000 dataset may compromise sensitivity toward less prevalent lesion categories.
Disparities in imaging attributes, such as quality, illumination, and resolution, may impair predictive performance.
Future improvements should address class imbalance, expand the training dataset, optimize deeper layers through fine-tuning, and evaluate state-of-the-art models like EfficientNet and ResNet.
Incorporating interpretability methods such as Grad-CAM and deploying the system on mobile or cloud infrastructure could facilitate instantaneous skin cancer detection in underserved settings.
important_remarks: The model achieved only 71.38% accuracy and 60.42% recall on the HAM10000 dataset, indicating limited sensitivity for detecting true positives, particularly for less prevalent classes.
Performance is likely compromised by class imbalance within the HAM10000 dataset and morphological similarities between lesion variants, which the authors acknowledge as key limitations.
The study lacks external validation; the model was trained and tested solely on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to heterogeneous clinical populations.
The paper does not report specific data augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, color jittering) or their parameters, limiting reproducibility of the preprocessing pipeline.
The training duration was very short (10 epochs with a batch size of 16), which may have hindered complete model convergence and optimal performance.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive metrics without inferential statistics.

The system is explicitly positioned as an auxiliary aid for preliminary screening and must not replace expert clinical evaluation, highlighting its current role as a decision-support tool rather than a diagnostic standard.

Deployment was limited to a Flask-based web application on a local Windows 10 environment with an Intel i5 CPU and 8 GB RAM, lacking evidence of robustness in diverse or resource-constrained clinical settings.

The paper mentions comparing against SVM and basic CNNs but does not provide detailed comparative metrics or statistical justification for the superiority of MobileNetV2 over other state-of-the-art models like EfficientNet or ResNet.
